name: Supervisor (Process Control System)
pricing_notes: Open source (BSD-like / Repoze license); Agendaless Consulting / Chris McDonough

auth.notes: Optional HTTP basic auth for supervisorctl and web interface; UNIX socket default

performance.notes: Control commands are near-instant; process startup time depends on application

agent_readiness.notes: UNIX process control system. Config: /etc/supervisor/conf.d/app.conf. [program:myapp] command=/usr/bin/python3 app.py. directory=/app. autostart=true. autorestart=true. stdout_logfile=/var/log/app.log. supervisord -c /etc/supervisor.conf. supervisorctl status. supervisorctl start myapp. supervisorctl stop myapp. supervisorctl restart myapp. supervisorctl tail myapp stdout. XML-RPC API: import xmlrpc.client. s = xmlrpc.client.ServerProxy('http://localhost:9001/RPC2'). s.supervisor.getProcessInfo('myapp'). s.supervisor.startProcess('myapp'). s.supervisor.getAllProcessInfo(). Web UI at :9001. Event listeners: PROCESS_STATE events. supervisord_log plugin ecosystem.

gotchas: Processes must not daemonize themselves — supervisor expects foreground processes
Log rotation is manual — logrotate config needed for long-running deployments
XML-RPC API is verbose — direct supervisorctl is preferred for scripts
No systemd integration — use systemd unit file for supervisord itself
Python 3 only in supervisor 4.x — supervisor 3.x was Python 2
best_when: Managing long-running Python processes (workers, servers) on Linux/Unix
Simple process supervision without systemd complexity
Keeping worker processes alive with autorestart
avoid_when: When systemd is available and preferred (more powerful, better logging)
summary: Supervisor (supervisord) manages UNIX processes. supervisorctl start/stop/restart/status. [program:name] config with autostart, autorestart, logging. XML-RPC API for programmatic control. BSD-like license by Agendaless. Best for Linux process supervision in non-containerized deployments. Processes must run in foreground. Not for Docker. No type annotations. Reliable for keeping workers alive. Prefer systemd for modern server setups.
